does: This survey comprehensively examines federated unlearning (FU), the problem of selectively removing client data and associated influence from federated learning models upon request. The paper synthesizes existing FU techniques and methodologies, establishing a unified workflow and framework for understanding how FL systems can implement data erasure while maintaining model utility. It identifies core challenges specific to the federated setting—including asynchronous client participation, decentralized model updates, and the need to unlearn information distributed across multiple clients—and catalogs various algorithmic approaches developed to address these challenges, from exact unlearning methods to approximate and efficient variants.
connects: This work builds on the intersection of machine unlearning and federated learning research. Machine unlearning emerged from data privacy regulations like GDPR's right to be forgotten, establishing the theoretical and practical foundations for selective forgetting in centralized models. Federated learning introduced distributed training that avoids centralizing raw data, creating a privacy-preserving paradigm. FU combines these streams by adapting unlearning mechanisms to federated settings, addressing the compounded complexity that federated architectures introduce. The survey connects to prior work on FL robustness, Byzantine-resilient aggregation, and differential privacy in federated systems, while acknowledging how decentralization complicates both the mechanics and verification of successful unlearning.
enables: This survey enables future development of privacy-respecting federated systems that can operationalize the right to be forgotten in distributed ML deployments. It clarifies design patterns and open problems that guide development of efficient FU algorithms, particularly those balancing computational cost against unlearning completeness and verification. The work points toward applications in regulated domains—healthcare, financial services, and personal AI systems—where both federated learning and data erasure guarantees are legally or ethically required. It also opens research directions in proving unlearning guarantees, designing lightweight unlearning protocols for resource-constrained clients, and developing auditable mechanisms that allow clients to verify their data has been truly forgotten from the global model.
